title: The Backbone of Impact Evaluation Methods
text:
About the book
This book is not intended to be a comprehensive textbook on impact evaluation and causal inference. Many excellent books and articles already provide rigorous and detailed treatments of causal inference, program evaluation, and econometric methods for policy analysis.
The purpose of this book is different. It is to go back to basics and offer a simple and transparent perspective on the logic behind the most widely used impact evaluation methods. Rather than emphasizing the technical aspects of evaluation methods, the book aims to clarify how each method constructs a counterfactual, what assumptions it requires, and how it works in practice, while keeping formal details to a minimum.

title: The Backbone of Impact Evaluation Methods
text:
How to estimate treatment effects?
The central question of ex-post impact evaluation is simple, and it is counterfactual: What would have happened to the outcome of interest in the absence of the treatment? The focus is usually on the units exposed to the treatment. For these units, we observe the post-treatment outcome, but we do not observe what their outcome would have been had they not been treated. The main challenge is therefore to estimate this missing counterfactual outcome. The difference between the observed post-treatment outcome and the estimated counterfactual outcome provides an estimate of the treatment effect of interest.



As in Sliding Doors, where Helen’s life splits into two possible paths, causal inference starts from the fact that only one realized outcome is observed, while the alternative path remains counterfactual.


Crucially, the counterfactual is never observed. To estimate treatment effects credibly, we therefore need to construct a plausible approximation of it. How can this be done? The most common approach, though not the only one, as discussed in Chapter 11, is to exploit information from untreated units. The basic idea is that untreated units can help approximate what would have happened to treated units in the absence of the treatment.
We will see how the most widely used evaluation strategies within this family of control-based counterfactual methods—randomized controlled trials (RCTs), matching methods, difference-in-differences (DiD), instrumental variables (IV), regression discontinuity (RD), and the synthetic control method (SCM)—exploit information from untreated units to retrieve the treatment effects of interest.
The central theme is transparency: showing how each method builds its counterfactual, from which units, and under which assumptions.

Transparency as the Guiding Principle of This Book
In recent years, transparency has become central to the debate surrounding machine and deep learning algorithms. Complex predictive models are often difficult to interpret, and it is not always clear how an algorithm transforms data into predictions. For this reason, researchers and practitioners increasingly ask for models that are not only accurate, but also interpretable and transparent. At the same time, much less attention is usually paid to transparency in the context of impact evaluation methods. It is often taken for granted that causal methods are transparent by nature. Yet, as discussed below, this is not necessarily true for some of the most commonly used approaches.
In a successful RCT, the logic of the comparison is usually easy to understand. Random assignment makes treated and untreated units comparable before the intervention: on average, the two groups are similar in all respects except for the treatment they receive. For example, in a trial testing a new drug, patients are randomly assigned either to receive the drug or to remain in the control group, so differences in health outcomes after treatment can be credibly attributed to the drug itself. As a result, the untreated group provides a natural benchmark for what would have happened to the treated group in the absence of the intervention. Similarly, in an RD design, the role of the comparison units is relatively intuitive. Units just below and just above the cutoff are compared because they are assumed to be similar, except for treatment assignment.
This clarity is often less immediate in other widely used methods. For instance, in a matching application, it is not always disclosed which untreated units are actually used to construct the counterfactual, how much weight each of them receives, and whether all treated units are retained in the final matched sample. In a staggered DiD design, the comparison group is often described simply as “untreated”, but this label can hide considerable complexity. Applied studies do not always make clear which untreated units drive the comparison, whether not-yet-treated units are part of the control group, and how much the estimate depends on specific groups or time periods. The same issue becomes even more complex in IV designs, especially when the instrument is non-binary, when multiple instruments are used, or when the empirical specification includes several fixed effects, covariates, and trends.






WarningTransparency



Impact evaluation methods may appear transparent at first sight. However, this apparent transparency can be misleading: the way in which they construct the counterfactual is not always clearly visible.
Understanding who is being compared with whom, and the assumptions under which the causal effects of interest can be identified, is essential for interpreting the estimates causally.


The lesson from SCM. One of the reasons why SCM has been so influential is that it makes a data-driven construction of the counterfactual clearly visible. SCM builds the counterfactual by choosing a weighted combination of untreated units through a sophisticated optimization algorithm, and this procedure is not necessarily transparent at first sight. However, once the optimization process is completed, the method reports the weights assigned to each control unit and usually presents a graph comparing the treated unit with its synthetic counterpart. This makes it possible to see which untreated units contributed to the counterfactual, how much they contributed, and whether the synthetic control provides a credible pre-treatment fit—a key condition for considering the estimated treatment effect as reliable.
This feature is not only useful from a methodological point of view; it is also pedagogically powerful. It reminds us that control-based impact evaluation methods ultimately rely on comparisons between treated and untreated units. They are not magic formulas that automatically identify the causal effect of interest; they are structured ways of making comparisons credible under specific assumptions.
No ground truth. This point is particularly important because in observational studies, we do not observe a ground truth causal effect. We observe what happened after a policy was introduced, but we do not observe what would have happened to the same units, at the same time, in the absence of that policy. This makes it difficult to compare evaluation strategies as we often compare machine learning algorithms for prediction. In prediction problems, models can be assessed against observed outcomes, and their accuracy can be compared directly. For example, algorithms predicting the sale prices of flats can later be evaluated by comparing their forecasts with the actual prices at which those flats were sold. In impact evaluation, instead, the counterfactual outcome is always missing. We therefore cannot simply run a “horse race” among methods and declare which one is empirically more accurate. For this reason, the credibility of an impact evaluation strategy depends mainly on the plausibility of its identifying assumptions and on the transparency of the comparison it constructs.






NoteWhy causality matters



Many important questions in economics, policy evaluation, business, political science, and the social sciences are causal questions. We do not only want to know whether two variables move together. We want to know whether changing one of them would actually change the other. This is the idea behind the familiar motto: association is not causation. Two variables may be strongly associated because one causes the other, but also because they are both driven by a third factor, because of selection, or simply because the observed relationship reflects a misleading comparison. Causal inference starts from this distinction.
Would a conditional cash transfer increase children’s school attendance? Would a public subsidy reduce workplace accidents? Would smaller classes improve student achievement? Would a carbon tax reduce firms’ energy consumption? In each case, the key question is not simply whether treated and untreated units differ. The key question is what would have happened to the same units in the absence of the treatment.
Estimating causal effects is difficult because this counterfactual outcome is not directly observed. We observe what happened after the policy change but not what would have happened otherwise. Impact evaluation methods are ways of making this missing comparison as credible as possible.
This is why causality is at the center of empirical work. Without a credible causal design, data can reveal patterns, correlations, and associations, but it cannot by itself tell us what works, for whom, and under which conditions.








WarningOne causal effect at a time



A causal effect is defined with respect to a specific intervention, outcome, target population, and counterfactual state of the world. For this reason, causal analysis should usually begin by clarifying one causal question at a time.
For example, suppose the outcome of interest is workers’ earnings. Asking whether a training program increases earnings is not the same as asking whether a wage subsidy, job-search assistance, or access to childcare increases earnings. The outcome is the same, but each question involves a distinct treatment, counterfactual comparison, and possibly target population. Mixing these questions in a single analysis can make the estimand unclear.
This matters because credible causal inference requires a precise answer to the question: compared to what? The same observed outcome may be consistent with many different causal stories. To estimate an effect, we need to define the treatment, the untreated state, the units of interest, the timing of the intervention, and the outcome to be evaluated.
Trying to estimate several causal effects at once often leads to ambiguity. It becomes difficult to understand which counterfactual is being constructed, which assumptions justify it, and what the estimated coefficient actually captures. A clear impact evaluation therefore starts by isolating one causal question, defining its counterfactual carefully, and then choosing an empirical strategy that can make that comparison credible.




The rest of the book
The second part of the book covers selected topics in impact evaluation, including:

Forecasting-based counterfactual methods. These methods estimate causal effects without necessarily requiring untreated units in the data. Instead, they construct the counterfactual by forecasting what would have happened to treated units in the absence of the treatment, and therefore rely on a different set of identifying assumptions.
Spillovers and interference. We will discuss what happens when the treatment affects not only treated units, but also the outcomes of some or all untreated units. In these settings, untreated units may no longer provide a ‘clean’ counterfactual.
Continuous treatments. Throughout most of the book, we focus on binary treatments, where units are either treated or untreated. This setup makes the counterfactual logic especially clear: the goal is to compare outcomes under treatment with outcomes in the absence of treatment. In this chapter we will consider more complex settings in which the treatment varies in intensity. In this case, the causal question is not only whether the treatment has an effect, but how the outcome changes as treatment intensity increases.







ImportantThe main message



Impact evaluation is not primarily about applying sophisticated estimators. It is about constructing a credible counterfactual scenario
Different methods provide different ways of approximating the missing counterfactual, and each of them relies on specific assumptions that should be assessed case by case, depending on the empirical setting, the available data, and the institutional context.


The book therefore does not try to cover every method, every extension, or every recent technical debate. Instead, it focuses on the basic logic of the main approaches and on the practical questions that arise when we use them to evaluate public policies.

title: The Backbone of Impact Evaluation Methods
text:
The audience of this book
The book is written for readers who want to understand the intuition behind impact evaluation methods before moving to technical details. It is intended for students, applied researchers, policy analysts, and practitioners who use causal methods or read studies based on them.
Formal notation is kept to a minimum and postponed to the end of each chapter. The emphasis is on interpretation, design, credibility, and transparency.

Disclaimer
This is version 0.1 of the book. It is still a work in progress, and I would be grateful for any comments, suggestions, or corrections that could help improve it.
All illustrations in this book were created with the assistance of a generative AI system, based on specific instructions and, in some cases, hand-drawn sketches provided by the author. They are intended for illustrative and educational purposes only.

title: 1  Why Impact Evaluation?
text:
1.1 An example: the opening of a nursery school
Does the opening of a nursery school in a neighborhood increase local employment? Do subsidies aimed at improving workplace safety actually reduce occupational injuries? Do local jurisdiction amalgamations affect voters’ political engagement? How many people died because of the COVID-19 pandemic?
These are all causal questions. They ask whether a specific policy, intervention, or shock produced a change in an outcome of interest. In the language of impact evaluation, we usually refer to this policy, intervention, or shock more generally as the treatment. The treatment may be the opening of a nursery school, the provision of workplace-safety subsidies, a municipal amalgamation, or a large unexpected shock such as the COVID-19 pandemic. The outcome may be parents’ employment, occupational injuries, voter turnout, or pandemic-related deaths.
But how can we answer such questions in a credible way?
Consider the first example. Suppose that, at time \(t_0\), a neighborhood has no nursery school. At time \(t_1\), a new nursery school opens. By time \(t_2\), we observe the employment status of parents of young children in that neighborhood.
Nursery schools may serve several purposes, from supporting child development to helping families manage care responsibilities. In this example, however, we focus on one specific mechanism: access to childcare may free up parents’ time and make it easier for them to participate in the labor market.
However, observing what happens after the nursery school opens is not enough to establish whether it caused a change in parents’ employment. To answer this question, we need to think in counterfactual terms.
This is the central difficulty of impact evaluation. We observe the employment status of parents in the treated neighborhood after the nursery school opens, but we cannot observe what their employment status would have been, at the same time, if the nursery school had not opened. The counterfactual is therefore missing. The goal of impact evaluation methods is to find a credible way to fill in this missing information.

title: 1  Why Impact Evaluation?
text:
NoteThe counterfactual question



What would have happened to parents’ employment in the treated neighborhood if the nursery school had not opened?

title: 1  Why Impact Evaluation?
text:
1.2 Three intuitive comparisons
There are several intuitive ways one might try to estimate the effect of the nursery school.
Option A: We could use a before–after comparison. We could compare parents’ employment in the neighborhood before the nursery school opened, at \(t_0\), with parents’ employment after the opening, at \(t_2\).
Option B: We could use a cross-sectional comparison. We could compare parents’ employment in the treated neighborhood after the nursery school opened, at \(t_2\), with parents’ employment in other neighborhoods where no nursery school opened during the same period.
Option C: We could use a difference-in-differences comparison. We could compare the change in parents’ employment in the treated neighborhood between \(t_0\) and \(t_2\) with the change in parents’ employment over the same period in neighborhoods that did not receive a new nursery school.
Each of these approaches is intuitive. Yet each can also be misleading.

title: 1  Why Impact Evaluation?
text:
1.3 Why simple comparisons may fail






NoteIdentification issues with simple comparisons






This vignette illustrates why the simple comparisons discussed above may fail to identify the causal effect of interest. Before-after comparisons may confound treatment effects with underlying time trends; cross-sectional comparisons may compare inherently different groups; and DiD may be misleading if parents’ employment was already evolving differently in treated and untreated areas before the treatment.




Option A: A before–after comparison may confuse the effect of the nursery school with other changes occurring over time. Parents’ employment may have increased even without the new facility. For example, the local labor market may already have been improving before the nursery school opened. In that case, attributing the entire increase in parents’ employment to the nursery school would overstate its effect. Similarly, if a negative economic shock occurred between \(t_1\) and \(t_2\), we might wrongly attribute the consequences of that shock to the nursery school.
Option B: A cross-sectional comparison may also lead to wrong conclusions. Neighborhoods with and without a new nursery school may differ in many ways even before the policy is introduced. They may have different income levels, demographic structures, education levels, labor-market opportunities, or demand for childcare. As these differences are usually related to employment, then comparing the two groups of parents after the nursery school opens would not correctly identify the employment effect of the nursery school itself.
Option C: A difference-in-differences comparison is the most sophisticated among these simple alternatives, but it still relies on an assumption that may not hold in this application. The key requirement is the parallel-trends assumption: if the nursery school had not opened, parents’ employment in the treated neighborhood would have evolved similarly to parents’ employment in the comparison neighborhoods. However, if parents’ employment was already evolving differently in the treated neighborhood before the opening, this assumption may not be credible. In that case, the estimated effect may still mix the impact of the nursery school with other pre-existing differences in parents’ employment trends.






ImportantBack to basics



The causal question is not whether the outcome changed over time, but whether it changed because of the treatment, and by how much.
To answer it, we need a credible counterfactual: a convincing approximation of what the outcome of treated units would have been in the post-treatment period had they not received the treatment.

title: 1  Why Impact Evaluation?
text:
1.4 Why observational data are challenging
Constructing a credible counterfactual is not easy, especially when working with observational data, as in the examples above. In observational settings, policies are not usually assigned at random. Nursery schools may open in neighborhoods with specific characteristics. Workplace-safety subsidies may be received by firms that are more proactive, more exposed to risk, or better informed about public programs. Municipal amalgamations may involve municipalities with particular administrative or political characteristics.
For this reason, a simple comparison between treated and untreated units may be misleading: it may reflect pre-existing differences between the two groups rather than the causal effect of the treatment

title: 1  Why Impact Evaluation?
text:
1.5 Why randomized controlled trials help
Estimating the causal effect would be easier in an RCT. Suppose, for example, that a large number of similar neighborhoods applied to receive a new nursery school, but budget constraints allowed the government to build only some of them.
If the neighborhoods receiving the new facility were chosen at random, then, on average, the treated and untreated neighborhoods would be comparable before the policy. This is because random assignment breaks the link between treatment status and the pre-existing characteristics of neighborhoods: richer and poorer areas, neighborhoods with higher or lower employment, and places with stronger or weaker demand for childcare would all have the same probability of receiving the new nursery school.
With a sufficiently large number of neighborhoods, the law of large numbers helps ensure that these characteristics are balanced between the treated and untreated groups. In that case, the neighborhoods without a new facility would provide a credible approximation of what would have happened to the treated neighborhoods in the absence of the policy.






NoteWhy randomization is powerful



Randomization is powerful because it makes the comparison group credible by design. Differences observed after the intervention can then be more plausibly attributed to the policy itself, rather than to pre-existing differences between treated and untreated units.
This is why RCTs are often considered the benchmark for causal evaluation.

title: 1  Why Impact Evaluation?
text:
1.6 Back to impact evaluation methods
In many policy contexts, however, randomization is not feasible. It may be too costly, politically unacceptable, ethically problematic, or simply impossible because the policy has already been implemented.
Much of impact evaluation therefore deals with a practical question:






TipThe practical question



How can we construct a credible counterfactual when random assignment is not available?


The methods discussed in this book can be seen as different answers to this question. Each method tries, in its own way, to approximate the missing counterfactual under certain assumptions. The key issue is not to choose the most sophisticated method, but to understand which available method is supported by the most credible assumptions in the empirical setting under analysis.






ImportantRCT thinking in observational studies



Donald Rubin puts the point very sharply:

“Valid frequentist causal inferences from an observational study can only be obtained if you embed the observational study within a hypothetical randomized experiment. […] Otherwise you are just looking at computer output and implied associations, and making up, generally silly, although sometimes interesting, stories.”

Source: This quote is from Rubin’s interview in Observational Studies (Rubin 2022).
This statement captures a central lesson of impact evaluation: even when we cannot run an RCT, we should still think as if we were trying to approximate one. This means asking what the corresponding randomized experiment would look like. If randomization had been possible, who would have been eligible for treatment? Which units would have been randomly assigned to treatment and control? What would the control condition have been?
In observational studies, we must then ask how the actual setting differs from this ideal experiment. Why did some units receive the treatment while others did not? Which pre-treatment characteristics may have influenced treatment assignment? Which untreated units are most comparable to the treated units? And under which assumptions can this observational comparison approximate the comparison that randomization would have created?
This way of thinking is useful because it disciplines the design of observational studies. It forces the researcher to move beyond the mechanical application of an evaluation methods and to make explicit the comparison that gives the causal estimate its meaning. In this sense, RCT thinking is not only relevant for randomized experiments. It is also a benchmark for observational studies: it helps clarify the target causal question, the assignment mechanism, the role of untreated units, and the assumptions needed to interpret an estimate causally.









Pearl and Mackenzie (2018) describe causal reasoning as a ladder of causality with three levels.



The ladder of causality distinguishes between observing associations, evaluating interventions, and reasoning about counterfactuals. The potential outcomes framework operates at the counterfactual level: it asks what would have happened to the same unit under a different treatment status.



The first level is association. At this level, we ask whether two variables move together. For example: are firms that receive public subsidies less likely to have workplace accidents? This is an associational question. It can be answered by comparing observed accident rates, or by estimating a regression that relates accident rates to subsidy receipt. However, it does not tell us whether the subsidy caused the difference. Many empirical analyses stop at this level. They document that a treatment variable is statistically associated with an outcome and then interpret the estimated coefficient as if it were a causal effect. This is tempting, but it is not enough. Unless we explain why the comparison between treated and untreated units approximates a credible counterfactual, the coefficient remains an association, not a causal estimate. One of the main aims of this book is to convince the reader that this is not a reliable way to estimate causal effects. To do so, we need to move up the ladder…
The second level is intervention. At this level, we ask what would happen if we actively changed something. For example: what would happen to workplace accidents if a firm received a public subsidy for safety investments? This is the level of causal effects and policy evaluation.
The third level is counterfactuals. At this level, we ask what would have happened under an alternative state of the world. For example: for a firm that received the subsidy, how many workplace accidents would have occurred if that same firm had not received it? This is the level of potential outcomes, because it compares the observed outcome with the missing counterfactual outcome.

In this sense, the potential outcomes framework works at the counterfactual level of the ladder. It defines causal effects by comparing what happened with what would have happened under a different treatment status.







Pearl, Judea, and Dana Mackenzie. 2018. The Book of Why: The New Science of Cause and Effect. Basic Books.


Rubin, Don. 2022. “Interview with Don Rubin.” Observational Studies 8 (2): 77–94. https://muse.jhu.edu/article/867089.

title: 2  The Potential Outcomes Framework
text:
2.1 Randomized Controlled Trials (RCTs)
The potential outcomes framework provides the basic language of causal inference. Its central idea is simple: to define a causal effect, we must compare what happens to the same unit under alternative treatment states.
The origins of this framework go back to the work of Jerzy Neyman on randomized agricultural experiments first published in 1927 (Neyman 1990). Neyman introduced the idea that each experimental unit has potential outcomes under different treatments, even though only one of them is observed. Later, Donald Rubin extended this logic beyond randomized experiments, turning it into a general framework for causal inference in both experimental and observational studies (Rubin 1974, 1978). For this reason, the approach is often called the Neyman–Rubin potential outcomes framework or the Rubin causal model.
Suppose we are interested in the effect of a binary treatment \(D_i\) on an outcome \(Y_i\) for unit \(i\), where:
\[
D_i =
\begin{cases}
1 & \text{if unit } i \text{ receives the treatment},\\
0 & \text{if unit } i \text{ does not receive the treatment}.
\end{cases}
\]
For each unit, we define two potential outcomes:
\[
Y_i(1)
\quad \text{and} \quad
Y_i(0).
\]
Here, \(Y_i(1)\) is the outcome that unit \(i\) would experience if treated, while \(Y_i(0)\) is the outcome that the same unit would experience if untreated. The individual treatment effect (ITE) is therefore
\[
\Delta_i = Y_i(1)-Y_i(0).
\]
This definition captures the essence of causal inference: a causal effect is the difference between two potential states of the same unit. For example, if the treatment is receiving a public subsidy aimed at reducing workplace accidents, the causal effect for a given firm is the difference between the number of workplace accidents with the subsidy at time \(t_2\) and the number of workplace accidents that the same firm would have experienced without the subsidy at time \(t_2\).
The problem is that we never observe both potential outcomes for the same unit. If the unit is treated, we observe \(Y_i(1)\) but not \(Y_i(0)\). If the unit is untreated, we observe \(Y_i(0)\) but not \(Y_i(1)\). The observed outcome can be written as
\[
Y_i^{obs} = D_iY_i(1) + (1-D_i)Y_i(0).
\]
This means that \(Y_i^{obs}=Y_i(1)\) for treated units, for which \(D_i=1\), and \(Y_i^{obs}=Y_i(0)\) for untreated units, for which \(D_i=0\). In other words, we observe only the potential outcome corresponding to the treatment status actually received.
The missing potential outcome is the counterfactual. This is why causal inference is fundamentally a missing-data problem: one potential outcome is observed, while the other must be inferred. Holland famously called this the fundamental problem of causal inference (Holland 1986).
Because individual treatment effects are generally impossible to observe, causal inference usually focuses on average treatment effects (discussed in Chapter 3).
The reason is that, although we cannot observe both potential outcomes for the same unit, we can observe different units under different treatment statuses. If treated and untreated units are ‘somewhat’ comparable, the average outcome among untreated units can be used to approximate the missing counterfactual outcome for treated units, and vice versa. In this sense, causal inference replaces the impossible comparison of the same unit under two alternative states of the world with a comparison between groups of units that are credible substitutes for one another, except for the treatment.
In randomized experiments, treatment assignment is controlled by the researcher. In a successful RCT, treatment status is independent of the units’ potential outcomes. Formally, random assignment implies
\[
(Y_i(1),Y_i(0)) \perp D_i.
\]
This means that treated and control groups are comparable on average, not necessarily unit by unit. The logic of randomization is therefore statistical. By the law of large numbers, as the number of units increases, random assignment tends to balance both observed and unobserved characteristics across treatment and control groups.
This also clarifies why randomizing only two units, one treated and one untreated, is not enough to make the RCT logic convincing. Even if treatment is randomly assigned, the two units may still differ substantially for reasons unrelated to the treatment. With only two units, there is no averaging that washes out individual idiosyncrasies or pre-existing differences.
With larger samples, instead, the average outcome of the control group provides an estimate of what the treated group would have experienced without treatment. Similarly, the average outcome of the treated group provides information about outcomes under treatment. In formal terms,
\[
E[Y_i(0)\mid D_i=1] = E[Y_i(0)\mid D_i=0].
\]
Randomization does not reveal the missing counterfactual for each individual unit. It solves the counterfactual problem on average, by creating a credible comparison group that reveals the missing average counterfactual.
This is why average treatment effects can be credibly estimated even when individual treatment effects cannot. RCTs are therefore often considered the benchmark for causal inference: by design, they solve the counterfactual problem for average treatment effects (Neyman 1990; Rubin 1978).

title: 2  The Potential Outcomes Framework
text:
2.2 Observational Studies
In many social science and policy settings, random assignment is not feasible. Treatment assignment is often shaped by choices, rules, or exposure conditions that are not controlled by the researcher. Individuals, firms, schools, or neighborhoods may select into treatment; policymakers may target specific units; and some units may be more likely to experience certain shocks because of their characteristics or location. Natural disasters provide a simple example: earthquakes are more likely to affect areas near fault lines, while hurricanes are more likely to hit coastal areas exposed to tropical storm paths.
As a result, treated and untreated units may differ even before the treatment occurs. Some of these differences may be observable, while others may be unobservable. If pre-existing differences also affect the outcome of interest, a simple post-treatment comparison may be misleading: it may capture not only the effect of the treatment, but also differences that were already present before treatment assignment.
This is why simple comparisons may suffer from selection bias.
It is also why many empirical researchers look for natural experiments. In a natural experiment, an institutional rule, a policy change, a threshold, timing, or an external shock generates variation in treatment that is plausibly unrelated to the units’ potential outcomes. This does not make the study automatically equivalent to an RCT, but it can bring observational data closer to the logic of random assignment. The hope is to exploit these quasi-random sources of variation to construct a more credible counterfactual and estimate causal parameters under more plausible assumptions.
Rubin’s contribution was to show that the same potential outcomes logic can be applied to observational studies (Rubin 1974). The goal is to reconstruct, as closely as possible, the comparison that a randomized experiment would have produced. Different causal methods do this in different ways. Matching, weighting, DiD, RDD, IV, and SCM all try to recover credible counterfactual outcomes by exploiting information from untreated units, but each relies on a different set of assumptions.

title: 2  The Potential Outcomes Framework
text:
2.3 Key Assumptions
The potential outcomes framework is not, by itself, an estimator. It defines causal effects and clarifies what must be observed, or what must be assumed, in order to estimate them. In its basic version, several assumptions are especially important and underlie all control-based evaluation strategies.

2.3.1 Manipulable treatment
The treatment must correspond to a clear intervention that could, at least in principle, be assigned or manipulated. This idea is often summarized by the motto: no causation without manipulation.
For example, attending a training program, receiving a subsidy, or gaining access to a nursery school can be interpreted as treatments. By contrast, characteristics such as nationality at birth or age are not treatments in the same direct sense, because they cannot be assigned by an intervention.






NoteGoing deeper



A related issue arises when a policy does not vary across units or over time. In principle, we can still imagine potential outcomes under alternative policy regimes. However, if every unit is always exposed to the same policy, and we never observe units under a different regime, there is no empirical comparison from which to learn what would have happened in the absence of that policy. In this case, the causal effect is not identified without additional and typically strong assumptions.




2.3.2 Consistency
Consistency means that the treatment we define in theory must be the same treatment that units actually receive in the data.
The observed outcome must correspond to the potential outcome associated with the treatment actually received. That is,
\[
Y_i^{obs} = Y_i(1) \quad \text{if } D_i=1,
\]
and
\[
Y_i^{obs} = Y_i(0) \quad \text{if } D_i=0.
\]
Consistency connects the observed data to the potential outcomes. Without it, the notation \(Y_i(1)\) and \(Y_i(0)\) would not map clearly into the outcomes observed in the data.


2.3.3 Stable Unit Treatment Value Assumption
A central assumption of the basic framework is the Stable Unit Treatment Value Assumption (SUTVA) (Rubin 1980). This assumption allows us to write only two potential outcomes for each unit: \(Y_i(1)\) and \(Y_i(0)\).
SUTVA has two main components.

There should be no hidden versions of the treatment. The treatment must be sufficiently homogeneous across treated units. If some units receive a light version of the program and others receive a much more intensive version, then the notation \(Y_i(1)\) is too vague. There is not one treatment, but several different treatments.
There should be no interference between units. The potential outcome of one unit should depend only on that unit’s own treatment status, not on the treatment status of other units. This idea is often traced back to Cox’s discussion of interference between experimental units (Cox 1958).

Formally, in the simple version of the framework, we write
\[
Y_i = Y_i(D_i).
\]
This notation assumes a binary treatment: unit \(i\)’s outcome depends only on its own treatment status \(D_i\), which can take only two values, 0 and 1. If instead unit \(i\)’s outcome also depends on whether other units are treated, the notation would need to be expanded.






WarningWhen SUTVA may fail



SUTVA may fail when the treatment has different versions or intensities, or when it generates spillovers, peer effects, displacement effects, or general equilibrium effects.


SUTVA can fail in two main ways:

Different versions or intensities of the treatment. Suppose that a school introduces a tutoring program for students with low test scores. Some students receive one-to-one tutoring, while others receive group tutoring. If we treat all participants as if they had received the same intervention, we are mixing different versions of the treatment. In this case, the estimated effect would not refer to a clearly defined program. A similar problem arises when the treatment varies in intensity. For example, students may receive different amounts of tutoring depending on their needs: some may attend only one hour per week, others three, five, seven, or even more. If we collapse all these different exposure levels into a single binary indicator of participation, the notation \(Y_i(1)\) becomes too vague. The problem is not that continuous treatments are incompatible with the potential outcomes framework. Rather, the treatment must be defined accordingly. For instance, we may write potential outcomes as \(Y_i(d)\), where \(d\) denotes treatment intensity. What violates the first component of SUTVA is treating different doses or versions as if they were the same treatment. (See Chapter 14 for a deeper discussion.)
The treatment received by one unit may affect the outcomes of other units. Suppose that students who receive tutoring share materials, explain exercises, or study together with classmates who were not selected for the program. In this case, untreated students are no longer unaffected by the intervention: their outcomes may improve because of their interaction with treated students. This violates the no-interference component of SUTVA. The comparison between tutored and non-tutored students would therefore be contaminated: the control group is not simply untreated, but indirectly exposed to the treatment. Whether control-based methods can still be used in similar scenarios depends on the extent and structure of the spillover effects. If spillovers affect only a clearly identifiable subset of untreated units, researchers may exclude those units from the control group or explicitly treat them as indirectly exposed. In this case, unaffected untreated units may still provide a credible counterfactual. By contrast, if spillovers reach all untreated units, there may be no ‘clean’ control group left, and standard control-based methods cannot recover the causal parameters of interest without additional strong assumptions. Importantly, spillover effects may also affect treated units. For example, tutored students may benefit not only from the tutoring program itself, but also from interactions with other tutored students. In this case, the causal effect of the treatment on the treated no longer captures only the direct effect of receiving the treatment. It may also include indirect effects generated by the treatment status of other units. It is therefore necessary to clarify exactly which causal effect is being estimated. (See Chapter 13 for a deeper discussion.)



2.3.4 Assignment mechanism
To estimate causal effects, we also need information about how treatment is assigned. In randomized experiments, assignment is random:
\[
(Y_i(1),Y_i(0)) \perp D_i.
\]
This means that treatment status is independent of the potential outcomes. In other words, units with high or low values of \(Y_i(1)\) or \(Y_i(0)\) are not systematically more likely to be assigned to treatment. As a result, the treated and control groups are comparable on average, and the control group can be used to estimate the missing counterfactual outcome for the treated group.
In observational studies, this is usually not true. For instance, let us consider the case of matching methods (see Chapter 5 for a deeper discussion.). The key identifying assumption is the conditional independence assumption (CIA):
\[
(Y_i(1),Y_i(0)) \perp D_i \mid X_i.
\]
This means that, after conditioning on pre-treatment or time-invariant observed covariates \(X_i\), treatment assignment is as good as random. In other words, among units with the same, or sufficiently similar, values of \(X_i\), treated and untreated units are comparable, and differences in their post-treatment outcomes can be interpreted as estimates of causal effects.
It is important to recognize that this assumption should generally be considered quite strong. It requires that all variables affecting both treatment assignment and potential outcomes are observed and correctly accounted for.
In other words, after conditioning on \(X_i\), there should be no remaining systematic differences between treated and untreated units that are related to the outcome. This is demanding because, in observational studies, treatment assignment often depends on factors that are difficult to measure. For example, workers may enter a training program because they are more motivated, firms may apply for subsidies because they have better managers, and neighborhoods may receive a policy because policymakers expect them to respond more strongly.
If these factors are unobserved and also affect the outcome, then treated and untreated units are still not comparable after conditioning on \(X_i\). In that case, CIA fails, and the estimated treatment effect may still reflect selection bias rather than the causal effect of the treatment.
A further difficulty is that, in observational studies, the assignment mechanism is often only partially understood. We usually do not know exactly why some units receive the treatment and others do not, or why they receive it at a particular time, place, or intensity. This makes it harder to assess whether the identifying assumptions behind a given evaluation strategy are plausible. For this reason, the assumptions used in observational studies should not be treated as mechanical conditions that can be satisfied by choosing a sophisticated estimator or adding many controls. They are substantive claims about how treatment assignment took place and about why the resulting comparison can be interpreted as a credible counterfactual.

title: 2  The Potential Outcomes Framework
text:
2.4 Summary






TipKey idea



Causal inference is not just about comparing outcomes.
It is about comparing observed outcomes with credible counterfactual outcomes.


The potential outcomes framework defines causal effects by comparing potential outcomes under treatment and no treatment. Its main contribution is to make the counterfactual problem explicit.
For each unit, the individual treatment effect is
\[
\Delta_i = Y_i(1)-Y_i(0),
\]
but only one of these two outcomes is observed. This makes individual causal effects generally impossible to measure directly.
Randomized experiments solve this problem on average by making treatment assignment independent of potential outcomes. Randomization does not make every treated unit identical to every untreated unit, but it makes the two groups comparable in expectation. Observational studies try to approximate this logic using assumptions and research designs that make treated and untreated units comparable.
The basic framework relies especially on SUTVA: the treatment must be well defined, and one unit’s outcome must not depend on the treatment status of other units. When these conditions fail, the simple notation \(Y_i(1)\) and \(Y_i(0)\) may no longer be sufficient, and the causal question must be reformulated more carefully.




Cox, David R. 1958. Planning of Experiments. John Wiley & Sons.


Holland, Paul W. 1986. “Statistics and Causal Inference.” Journal of the American Statistical Association 81 (396): 945–60. https://doi.org/10.1080/01621459.1986.10478354.


Neyman, Jerzy. 1990. “On the Application of Probability Theory to Agricultural Experiments: Essay on Principles. Section 9.” Statistical Science 5 (4): 465–72. https://doi.org/10.1214/ss/1177012031.


Rubin, Donald B. 1974. “Estimating Causal Effects of Treatments in Randomized and Nonrandomized Studies.” Journal of Educational Psychology 66 (5): 688–701. https://doi.org/10.1037/h0037350.


Rubin, Donald B. 1978. “Bayesian Inference for Causal Effects: The Role of Randomization.” The Annals of Statistics 6 (1): 34–58. https://doi.org/10.1214/aos/1176344064.


Rubin, Donald B. 1980. “Comment on ‘Randomization Analysis of Experimental Data: The Fisher Randomization Test’.” Journal of the American Statistical Association 75 (371): 591–93.

title: 3  Causal Parameters of Interest
text:
3.1 The most common causal parameters of interest: ATE and ATT
The starting point of causal inference is the idea that a treatment can modify an outcome for a given unit. Let \(D_i\) denote treatment status for unit \(i\), with \(D_i=1\) if the unit is treated and \(D_i=0\) otherwise. For each unit, we define two potential outcomes:
\[
Y_i(1) \quad \text{and} \quad Y_i(0),
\]
where \(Y_i(1)\) is the outcome that unit \(i\) would experience under treatment, and \(Y_i(0)\) is the outcome that the same unit would experience without treatment.
The individual treatment effect (ITE) is therefore
\[
\Delta_i = Y_i(1) - Y_i(0).
\]
This is the most direct definition of a causal effect: it compares the same unit in two alternative states of the world. For example, it asks how the employment status of the same individual would differ if she attended a training program rather than not attending it.
The difficulty is that, for each unit, only one of the two potential outcomes is observed. If \(D_i=1\), we observe \(Y_i(1)\) but not \(Y_i(0)\). If \(D_i=0\), we observe \(Y_i(0)\) but not \(Y_i(1)\). The observed outcome can be written as
\[
Y_i^{obs} = D_iY_i(1) + (1-D_i)Y_i(0).
\]
The missing potential outcome is the counterfactual outcome. This is the fundamental problem of causal inference: it is generally impossible to observe the causal effect of treatment for a single unit.
Because ITEs are usually difficult, if not impossible, to estimate accurately, causal inference typically focuses on average causal effects. The most general parameter is the average treatment effect (ATE):
\[
ATE = E[\Delta_i] = E[Y_i(1)-Y_i(0)].
\]
Equivalently,
\[
ATE = E[Y_i(1)] - E[Y_i(0)].
\]
The ATE answers the question: what is the expected effect of the treatment for a randomly selected unit from the population? Using the training program example, the ATE measures the expected effect of the program for a randomly selected individual from the population.
However, the ATE is usually not the most relevant parameter for policy evaluation. A program is generally designed for a specific group, rather than for the whole population. In that case, we are often more interested in the average treatment effect on the treated (ATT):
\[
ATT = E[\Delta_i \mid D_i=1].
\]
Equivalently,
\[
ATT = E[Y_i(1) \mid D_i=1] - E[Y_i(0) \mid D_i=1].
\]
The ATT answers the question: what is the expected effect of the treatment for units that actually received the treatment? Using the training program example, the ATT measures the expected effect of the program for individuals who actually participated in it. Therefore, the ATT is especially useful when evaluating whether a program generated benefits for its participants.
The first component, \(E[Y_i(1) \mid D_i=1]\), is observed in the data:
\[
E[Y_i(1) \mid D_i=1] = E[Y_i^{obs} \mid D_i=1].
\]
The second component, \(E[Y_i(0) \mid D_i=1]\), is not observed. It is the mean outcome that treated units would have experienced without treatment. Estimating this counterfactual is the central empirical challenge.

title: 3  Causal Parameters of Interest
text:
WarningThe difficulties in estimating \(E[Y_i(0) \mid D_i=1]\)



The simplest way to compare treated and untreated units is the difference-in-means (DIM) estimator. It is defined as the difference between the average observed outcome of treated units and the average observed outcome of untreated units:
\[
DIM = E[Y_i^{obs} \mid D_i=1] - E[Y_i^{obs} \mid D_i=0].
\]
But, in general,
\[
E[Y_i(0) \mid D_i=1] \neq E[Y_i(0) \mid D_i=0].
\]
The difference
\[
\text{Bias} = E[Y_i(0) \mid D_i=1] - E[Y_i(0) \mid D_i=0]
\]
is usually referred to as selection bias. Therefore,
\[
DIM = ATT + Bias.
\]
This shows why a simple comparison between treated and untreated units does not generally identify the ATT.

title: 3  Causal Parameters of Interest
section: 3.2 Other causal parameters of interest: LATE and ITT
text:
3.2 Other causal parameters of interest: LATE and ITT
Besides the ATE and ATT, two other causal parameters are especially important in experimental and quasi-experimental settings: the local average treatment effect (LATE) and the intention-to-treat effect (ITT).
The local average treatment effect (LATE). LATE is the average treatment effect for the subset of units whose treatment status is changed by an instrument or an assignment rule. In an IV setting, for example, the LATE is the effect of treatment for units whose treatment status is changed by the instrument. These units are usually called compliers. A similar logic applies in an RD design. Suppose that students become eligible for a scholarship only if their test score is above a given cutoff. The LATE is the effect of the scholarship for students close to the cutoff whose treatment status is changed by the eligibility rule. It is therefore a local effect: it applies to units near the threshold, not necessarily to all students. The LATE is important because many empirical designs do not identify the effect for the whole targeted population. Instead, they identify the effect for the units whose behavior is shifted by the source of exogenous variation (that is, a factor that changes treatment status but is otherwise unrelated to what the units’ outcomes would have been). This means that the estimated effect may depend on the specific instrument or assignment rule being used.
The intention-to-treat effect (ITT). ITT measures the effect of being assigned or offered treatment, regardless of whether units actually receive or comply with the treatment. It is especially relevant in randomized experiments with imperfect compliance. For policy purposes, the ITT is often meaningful because it captures the effect of implementing an offer, eligibility rule, or assignment mechanism in the real world. For example, suppose a development program randomly offers farmers a voucher for improved seeds and fertilizer, but only some farmers actually redeem the voucher and use the inputs. The ITT compares the average outcomes of farmers who were offered the voucher with those who were not, regardless of actual take-up. It therefore estimates the effect of the policy offer itself, rather than the effect of actually using the improved inputs.

title: 3  Causal Parameters of Interest
text:
3.3 Conditional average treatment effects
In many applications, treatment effects may also vary across groups. We may then be interested in heterogeneous treatment effects, usually summarized by a conditional average treatment effect (CATE):
\[
CATE(x) = E[Y_i(1)-Y_i(0) \mid X_i=x].
\]
For example, a training program may be more effective for younger workers than for older workers, or a childcare policy may have stronger employment effects among mothers with young children.
Similarly, one can define a conditional ATT (CATT):
\[
CATT(x) =
E[Y_i(1)-Y_i(0) \mid D_i=1, X_i=x].
\]
These conditional parameters are useful because they tell us not only whether a policy works on average, but also for whom it works best. (See Chapter 12 for a deeper discussion.)

title: 4  Randomized Controlled Trials
text:
4.1 What is an RCT?
A randomized controlled trial (RCT) is the benchmark research design for causal inference. It is so central to the logic of causal analysis that we have already briefly discussed it in each of the previous chapters. Its logic is simple: if treatment is assigned randomly, treated and untreated units should be comparable on average before the intervention. Therefore, any systematic difference in outcomes after the intervention can be attributed to the treatment rather than to pre-existing differences between the two groups.
In the potential outcomes framework, each unit \(i\) has two potential outcomes:
\[
Y_i(1)
\quad \text{and} \quad
Y_i(0),
\]
where \(Y_i(1)\) is the outcome under treatment and \(Y_i(0)\) is the outcome without treatment. The individual treatment effect is
\[
\Delta_i = Y_i(1)-Y_i(0).
\]
As discussed earlier, we cannot observe both potential outcomes for the same unit. Randomized experiments solve this problem statistically: they create a treated group and a control group that are comparable in expectation.

title: 4  Randomized Controlled Trials
text:
NoteKey idea



An RCT creates a credible counterfactual by random assignment.
The control group tells us what would have happened to the treated group, on average, in the absence of treatment.



4.1.1 Random Assignment
Let \(D_i\) be the treatment indicator:
\[
D_i =
\begin{cases}
1 & \text{if unit } i \text{ is assigned to treatment},\\
0 & \text{if unit } i \text{ is assigned to control}.
\end{cases}
\]
In a randomized experiment, \(D_i\) is assigned by chance. Conceptually, this is like flipping a coin: before assignment, each eligible unit has a known probability (in most cases 50%) of being allocated to the treatment or control group. In practice, randomization is usually implemented through more structured procedures, such as computer-generated random numbers, stratified randomization, or block randomization. The key point, however, is the same: treatment assignment is governed by a known random process, rather than by the choices or characteristics of the units. This implies that treatment status is independent of the potential outcomes:
\[
(Y_i(1),Y_i(0)) \perp D_i.
\]
This condition is crucial. It means that treated and control units do not differ systematically in terms of the outcomes they would have had under treatment or under control. Any difference between the two groups is due only to random sampling variation.
As a result,
\[
E[Y_i(0)\mid D_i=1] = E[Y_i(0)\mid D_i=0],
\]
and
\[
E[Y_i(1)\mid D_i=1] = E[Y_i(1)\mid D_i=0].
\]
The control group can therefore be used to estimate the missing counterfactual outcome for the treated group.


4.1.2 Estimating the Treatment Effect
In the simplest randomized experiment, the treatment effect can be estimated using a difference in mean outcomes:
\[
\widehat{\tau}_{RCT}
=
\overline{Y}^{obs}_{D=1}
-
\overline{Y}^{obs}_{D=0}.
\]
That is, we compare the average observed post-treatment outcome among treated units with the average observed post-treatment outcome among control units.
Because treatment is randomly assigned,
\[
E[Y_i^{obs}\mid D_i=1] = E[Y_i(1)],
\]
and
\[
E[Y_i^{obs}\mid D_i=0] = E[Y_i(0)].
\]
Therefore,
\[
E[\widehat{\tau}_{RCT}]
=
E[Y_i(1)] - E[Y_i(0)]
=
ATE.
\]
This is the central strength of randomized experiments: a simple comparison of means identifies the ATE, provided that the experiment is properly implemented.






NoteWhy randomization matters



Without randomization, treated and untreated units may differ for many reasons other than the treatment.
With randomization, these differences are balanced on average, so the control group provides a credible counterfactual.




4.1.3 Randomization and Selection Bias
In observational studies, treated and untreated units often differ before the treatment. This creates selection bias. A simple difference in means can be written as
\[
DIM =
E[Y_i^{obs}\mid D_i=1]
-
E[Y_i^{obs}\mid D_i=0].
\]
In general,
\[
DIM = ATT + Bias,
\]
where
\[
Bias =
E[Y_i(0)\mid D_i=1]
-
E[Y_i(0)\mid D_i=0].
\]
In a randomized experiment, random assignment implies
\[
E[Y_i(0)\mid D_i=1]
=
E[Y_i(0)\mid D_i=0].
\]
Therefore,
\[
Bias = 0.
\]
This is why randomized controlled trials are often described as the gold standard of causal inference: they remove selection bias by design.


4.1.4 Treatment and Control Groups
An RCT requires at least two groups:

a treatment group, which receives the intervention;
a control group, which does not receive the intervention.

The control group is not a secondary detail. It is what allows us to approximate the counterfactual outcome: what would have happened to the treated units if they had not received the treatment.
For example, suppose we want to evaluate whether sending households personalized feedback on their energy consumption reduces electricity use. We randomly assign eligible households either to receive the feedback messages or not to receive them. After the intervention, we compare electricity consumption across the two groups. If randomization was successful, the difference in consumption can be interpreted as the causal effect of the feedback intervention.


4.1.5 Why Experiments Are Powerful
Randomized experiments are powerful because treatment assignment is under the researcher’s control. Units are assigned to treatment and control groups according to a known procedure. This avoids the main problem of many observational studies: units self-select into treatment or are selected by policymakers in ways that may be related to the outcome.
For instance, people who choose to attend a training course may be more motivated than those who do not. Firms that apply for subsidies may have better growth prospects than firms that do not apply. Neighborhoods receiving a new nursery school may already differ from other neighborhoods in income, demographics, or demand for childcare.
Randomization breaks this link between treatment assignment and potential outcomes because units are assigned to treatment or control before the treatment takes place. It creates treated and untreated groups that are comparable before the treatment, at least in expectation.






NoteThe Project STAR experiment



One of the largest and most influential RCTs in education is Project STAR (Student/Teacher Achievement Ratio), conducted in Tennessee in the 1980s.
The experiment randomly assigned students and teachers to different classroom types: small classes, regular-size classes, and regular-size classes with a teacher aide. Because assignment was randomized, differences in later outcomes across these groups can be interpreted as causal effects of class size, under the usual assumptions of the randomized design.
Project STAR is especially important because it studied a policy question of direct relevance for education policy: whether reducing class size improves student achievement. The results showed that students assigned to smaller classes performed better on standardized tests, especially in the early grades. Later research also found that the benefits of small classes persisted over time, affecting outcomes such as test scores, college attendance, and earnings.
This example illustrates the strength of RCTs. Randomization creates comparable groups before treatment, so the control group provides a credible estimate of what would have happened to students assigned to small classes if they had instead attended regular-size classes.
At the same time, Project STAR also shows that even large RCTs require careful interpretation. Researchers must consider issues such as compliance, attrition, implementation quality, and whether the results can be generalized to other schools, states, or education systems.

title: 4  Randomized Controlled Trials
text:
4.2 Historical Intuition
The logic of randomization was developed first in the context of experimental statistics. Fisher’s work on agricultural experiments showed how random assignment could be used to separate the effect of a treatment from background variation in the environment (Fisher 1935). Neyman’s earlier work on potential outcomes also provided a formal way to think about causal effects as comparisons between different possible outcomes for the same unit (Neyman 1990).
The idea later became central in medicine. A landmark example is the 1948 randomized trial of streptomycin for pulmonary tuberculosis, conducted by the Medical Research Council in the United Kingdom (Medical Research Council 1948). Patients were allocated to treatment or control groups using randomization, and outcomes were compared across groups. This design helped establish the randomized controlled trial as a benchmark for evaluating whether a treatment works.






NoteHow RCTs transformed medicine



RCTs revolutionized the medical sector because they changed how evidence on treatments is produced. Before RCTs became standard, medical practice often relied on clinical experience, expert judgment, biological reasoning, or uncontrolled comparisons. These sources of knowledge can be valuable, but they can also be misleading when patients receiving a treatment differ systematically from those who do not.
Randomization made it possible to compare treatments in a more credible way. If patients are randomly assigned to a new therapy or to a control condition, differences in health outcomes can be attributed more convincingly to the treatment rather than to pre-existing differences in disease severity, age, lifestyle, or physician choices.
This logic became the foundation of evidence-based medicine. Modern drug approval, clinical guidelines, and many public health recommendations rely heavily on randomized evidence. RCTs also made clear that plausible treatments can fail, and that some treatments believed to be effective may have little benefit or even cause harm.
The broader lesson for impact evaluation is the same: a credible evaluation requires a credible counterfactual. Medicine adopted RCTs because they provide the cleanest ways to construct that counterfactual.

title: 4  Randomized Controlled Trials
text:
4.3 A/B tests
An A/B test is a simple form of randomized experiment, commonly used in digital platforms, marketing, and product design.
Users are randomly assigned to one of two versions of an intervention. In practice, this randomization is usually implemented automatically by the digital platform: when a user visits the website or opens the app, an algorithm assigns the user to one version or the other, often using a random number generator linked to the user ID.

Group A sees the original version;
Group B sees a modified version.

For example, an online platform may randomly show two versions of a webpage. Version A has the original layout, while version B changes the color or position of a button. The outcome may be the click rate, conversion rate, purchase probability, or time spent on the page.
Because users are randomly assigned to A or B, the difference in average outcomes can be interpreted causally:
\[
\widehat{\tau}_{AB}
=
\overline{Y}^{obs}_{B}
-
\overline{Y}^{obs}_{A}.
\]
The key point is that the two groups should be comparable before seeing the webpage. Therefore, if version B produces a higher conversion rate, the difference can be attributed to the change being tested, rather than to pre-existing differences between users.

title: 4  Randomized Controlled Trials
text:
4.4 Threats to Internal Validity






WarningRCTs are not magic



Randomization removes selection bias by design, but the experiment can still be threatened by failed randomization, non-compliance, attrition, behavioral responses, or small samples.


Although randomized experiments are the gold standard in causal inference, random assignment alone does not automatically guarantee a valid causal estimate. Several problems can threaten the internal validity of an RCT, meaning the credibility of the claim that the estimated effect for the study population is caused by the treatment itself.

Failure of Randomization. Randomization may fail if assignment is not truly random or if the randomization procedure is poorly implemented. For example, assigning units based on surname initials may appear unrelated to the outcome, but it can create imbalances if surname initials are correlated with ethnicity, geography, or socioeconomic status. A first diagnostic step is therefore to check whether pre-treatment characteristics are balanced across treatment and control groups. More generally, in an RCT treatment status should be assigned through a genuine random procedure, rather than through informal, discretionary, or ad hoc allocation rules.
Non-compliance. A second problem is non-compliance. Some units assigned to treatment may not actually receive the treatment, while some units assigned to control may gain access to it. In this case, we must distinguish between:


assignment to treatment;
actual treatment received. The effect of assignment is often called the intention-to-treat effect:

\[
ITT =
E[Y_i^{obs}\mid W_i=1]
-
E[Y_i^{obs}\mid W_i=0],
\]
where \(W_i\) denotes assignment to treatment. The ITT measures the effect of being offered or assigned the treatment, regardless of whether units actually comply. This can be highly relevant for policy, because real-world programs often face imperfect take-up.

Attrition. Attrition occurs when some units leave the study before outcomes are measured. Attrition is especially problematic if it is related to treatment status or potential outcomes. For example, if the weakest students are more likely to drop out of a schooling experiment, the final sample may no longer be comparable across treatment and control groups. In that case, the estimated effect may reflect both the treatment and selective dropout.
Behavioral Responses. Participants may change their behavior simply because they know they are part of an experiment. This is often called a Hawthorne effect. The term comes from a series of studies conducted at the Hawthorne Works factory in Cicero (Illinois), where researchers observed that workers’ behavior and productivity changed partly because they were being studied. Similarly, researchers or administrators may behave differently toward treated and control units if they know who receives the treatment. In medical trials, double-blind placebo designs are often used to reduce these problems.
Small Samples. Randomization balances treated and control groups on average. However, in small samples, random imbalance can still occur. Treated and control units may differ by chance in important pre-treatment characteristics. For this reason, even in randomized experiments, researchers usually report balance checks and may adjust for pre-treatment covariates to improve precision.

title: 4  Randomized Controlled Trials
text:
4.5 Threats to External Validity
RCTs are strong on internal validity, but they do not automatically answer every policy question. An experiment may identify the causal effect for a particular population, period, and implementation setting. The effect may differ elsewhere.
For example, a recycling campaign tested in one city may not have the same effect in another city with different waste-collection infrastructure or different social norms around recycling. An energy-saving intervention tested among households with smart meters may work less well when scaled up to areas where households do not receive real-time feedback on their electricity consumption.
This is the issue of external validity: whether the estimated effect generalizes beyond the experimental setting.
External validity is especially important when RCT evidence is used to guide policy at scale. A trial may be implemented by highly motivated researchers, with careful monitoring, well-trained staff, and selected participants. Once the same intervention is implemented by ordinary institutions, with larger and more heterogeneous populations, the effect may change. For this reason, policymakers should ask not only whether the intervention worked in the experiment, but also whether the mechanisms, incentives, constraints, and implementation capacity are similar in the setting where the policy will be applied.






WarningExternal validity in medical RCTs



External validity is a central issue in medical RCTs. A drug may be shown to be effective and safe for the population included in a trial, but this does not automatically imply that the same conclusion applies to all patients.
Clinical trials often restrict eligibility. They may exclude children, pregnant women, older patients, people with multiple health conditions, or patients taking other medications. These restrictions can improve internal validity and patient safety during the trial, but they also limit the population to which the results directly apply.
This is one reason why some drugs are not recommended, or are not authorized, for children below a certain age. The problem is not necessarily that the drug is known to be ineffective or harmful for those children. Rather, it may be that the drug has not been adequately tested in that age group. Since children differ from adults in body weight, metabolism, development, and possible side effects, evidence from adult trials cannot always be extrapolated safely.

title: 4  Randomized Controlled Trials
text:
4.6 Summary
RCTs provide the cleanest design for estimating causal effects. By randomly assigning units to treatment and control groups, they make treatment status independent of potential outcomes:
\[
(Y_i(1),Y_i(0)) \perp D_i.
\]
This allows the researcher to estimate the ATE using a simple difference in mean outcomes:
\[
\widehat{\tau}_{RCT}
=
\overline{Y}^{obs}_{D=1}
-
\overline{Y}^{obs}_{D=0}.
\]
The strength of RCTs is that they solve the selection problem by design. The limitation is that they can be difficult to implement in social science and policy settings, and they remain vulnerable to practical threats such as non-compliance, attrition, behavioral responses, and limited external validity.




Fisher, Ronald A. 1935. The Design of Experiments. Oliver; Boyd.


Medical Research Council. 1948. “Streptomycin Treatment of Pulmonary Tuberculosis: A Medical Research Council Investigation.” British Medical Journal 2 (4582): 769–82. https://doi.org/10.1136/bmj.2.4582.769.


Neyman, Jerzy. 1990. “On the Application of Probability Theory to Agricultural Experiments: Essay on Principles. Section 9.” Statistical Science 5 (4): 465–72. https://doi.org/10.1214/ss/1177012031.

title: 5  Selection on observable methods
text:
5.1 The linear regression model
Selection-on-observables methods rely on the idea that treatment assignment is non-random, but that the factors driving selection into treatment are observed in the data. If all confounders are observed and measured before treatment, where confounders are variables that jointly affect treatment assignment and potential outcomes, then differences between treated and untreated units can be adjusted for using pre-treatment covariates. Regression models implement this logic by adjusting outcome comparisons through control variables, while matching methods implement it by directly comparing treated units with untreated units that are similar in terms of observed pre-treatment characteristics. Both approaches therefore rely on the same central assumption: after conditioning on observables, there are no remaining unobserved confounders driving selection into treatment.
The linear regression model is probably the most prominent tool in statistics and econometrics. In its simplest form, it describes the relationship between an outcome variable and one explanatory variable. In causal inference, this same tool can be used to estimate treatment effects, but only under specific assumptions. Dozens of excellent textbooks explain linear regression in detail, covering its statistical foundations, estimation, inference, diagnostics, and extensions. This chapter does not aim to provide a full treatment of the model. Instead, it focuses on its role in causal inference when embedded in the counterfactual approach. The key question is not only how to estimate a regression coefficient, but under what conditions that coefficient can be interpreted as a causal effect.
Suppose we observe a post-treatment outcome \(Y_i\) and a binary treatment indicator \(D_i\), where \(D_i=1\) for treated units and \(D_i=0\) for untreated units. A simple regression model can be written as
\[
Y_i = \alpha + \tau D_i + u_i.
\]
In this model, \(\alpha\) is the average outcome for untreated units, while \(\tau\) captures the difference in average outcomes between treated and untreated units. The term \(u_i\) is the residual: the part of the outcome that is not explained by treatment status.
The parameters \(\alpha\) and \(\tau\) are usually estimated by ordinary least squares (OLS). In simple terms, OLS chooses the values of \(\alpha\) and \(\tau\) that make the predicted outcomes as close as possible to the observed outcomes, by minimizing the sum of squared residuals.
With a binary treatment and an intercept, the OLS estimate of \(\tau\) is equal to the difference in sample means between treated and untreated units:
\[
\widehat{\tau}_{OLS}
=
\bar{Y}_{D=1} - \bar{Y}_{D=0}.
\]
At the population level, this corresponds to
\[
\tau = E[Y_i \mid D_i=1] - E[Y_i \mid D_i=0].
\]
This coefficient is simply a difference in mean outcomes between treated and untreated units. It has a causal interpretation only if this observed difference reproduces the comparison we would like to make between potential outcomes. This requires treatment assignment to be unrelated to potential outcomes, as in a randomized experiment.
In observational studies, treated and untreated units often differ for reasons that are not random. In these cases, the simple regression coefficient \(\tau\) does not necessarily isolate the causal effect of the treatment. It may combine the treatment effect with pre-existing differences between the two groups.
For this reason, simple regression is rarely enough to identify causal effects in observational settings. A more useful starting point is the multiple regression model:
\[
Y_i = \alpha + \tau D_i + X_i'\beta + u_i,
\]
where \(Y_i\) is a post-treatment outcome, \(D_i\) is the treatment indicator, and \(X_i\) is a vector of observed pre-treatment covariates. These covariates should include variables that affect both treatment assignment and the outcome.
The timing of the variables is crucial. The covariates in \(X_i\) must be measured before treatment occurs, while the outcome \(Y_i\) must be measured after treatment. Otherwise, we may control for variables that are themselves affected by the treatment, changing the interpretation of the regression coefficient.
In this model, \(\tau\) compares treated and untreated units holding constant the covariates included in \(X_i\). The idea is that, after adjusting for relevant observed pre-treatment differences, the remaining difference in post-treatment outcomes may provide a credible estimate of the causal effect. For this interpretation to hold, several conditions are required.
For the ATT, the key selection-on-observables assumption is that, conditional on \(X_i\), treated and untreated units would have had comparable untreated potential outcomes:
\[
Y_i(0) \perp D_i \mid X_i.
\]
This means that, after conditioning on observed pre-treatment covariates, untreated units can be used to approximate the missing counterfactual outcomes of treated units. Regression implements this idea by estimating the relationship between \(Y_i\), \(D_i\), and \(X_i\), and using it to adjust for observed differences in covariates.
In the end, it is important to remember that OLS regression is not born causal. It becomes a causal tool only when embedded in a credible research design and supported by assumptions that are often quite demanding. Without these assumptions, the estimated coefficient remains a conditional association, not a causal effect.

title: 5  Selection on observable methods
text:
All relevant confounders must be observed and adequately controlled for. If a variable affects both selection into treatment and the outcome but is omitted from the model, the OLS estimate may still be biased. This is the problem of omitted variable bias.
Covariates must be measured before treatment occurs. If we control for variables affected by the treatment, we may remove part of the treatment effect or introduce additional bias.
There must be sufficient overlap between treated and untreated units. For treated units, there should be comparable untreated units with similar values of the observed covariates. Without common support, the regression relies heavily on extrapolation.
The regression specification must be credible. OLS is a parametric adjustment method: it imposes a functional form for how covariates are related to the outcome. If this functional form is too restrictive, for example because important nonlinearities or interactions are omitted, the estimate may still be misleading.










NoteWorked example: speed cameras and road accidents



Suppose we want to estimate whether installing speed cameras reduces the number of road accidents. The unit of analysis is a road segment. The treatment variable \(D_i\) is equal to 1 if road segment \(i\) receives a speed camera, and 0 otherwise. The outcome \(Y_i\) is the number of accidents observed after the installation period.
A first approach would be to estimate a simple regression model:
\[
Y_i =
\alpha
+
\tau D_i
+
u_i.
\]
In this model, \(\tau\) is simply the difference in average post-treatment accidents between roads with speed cameras and roads without speed cameras. This comparison would probably be misleading.
